title: A First Look at Flutter's Appearance and Features
description: Flutter Basics Overview: UI Architecture, State Management, and Toolchain Practices, Getting Started and Common Pitfalls.
content:
## Background

Flutter is Google's mobile UI framework, enabling the rapid creation of high-quality native user interfaces on iOS and Android. It's written in Dart.

## UI

At the UI layer, the Flutter framework encapsulates a custom UI structure. Individual Views become Widgets, and Views are distinguished by state, categorized as stateless or stateful.

## Framework

The Flutter framework, according to official documentation, provides several design schemes. In line with Flutter's background, the entire framework is based on the state pattern, specifically state management. As mentioned in Flutter, this is divided into ephemeral state and app state. Simply put, ephemeral state refers to Flutter's business logic, such as user button clicks updating logic and UI; app state refers to the app's own state, such as receiving message notifications.

An interesting point of discussion emerged regarding state design. For example,

When asked about React’s `setState` versus Redux’s store, the author of Redux, Dan Abramov, replied:

“The rule of thumb is: Do whatever is less awkward.”

[Original text](https://github.com/reduxjs/redux/issues/1287#issuecomment-175351978)

To make it easier to get started, the official documentation recommends some excellent Flutter frameworks and provides design implementation examples. [Link](https://flutter.dev/docs/development/data-and-backend/state-mgmt/options).

## Localization

Localization is relatively simple. Flutter provides a set of utility classes, similar to the current NSLocalization.

## [Tools](https://flutter.dev/docs/development/tools) & Debug

Flutter's development environment is a separate environment. IDEs, VS (VS Code), and AS (Android Studio) can install the corresponding plugs.

Debugging is divided into command-line and custom methods.

Command-line debugging is performed by installing the Dart Command Line.

Custom debugging currently supports debugging on Android Studio, but iOS debugging is not yet supported in Apple's tools such as Xcode.

The view interface is currently browser-based; code scanning relies on Dart's scanning tools. Overall, it's not integrated into a unified environment, resulting in a steep learning curve in the early stages. Unexpected development situations could cause developers to spend a significant amount of time troubleshooting. This needs improvement.

## Conclusion

In summary, Flutter is a completely new platform (beyond Web and Native), based on the underlying Skia graphics engine (https://skia.org/) for rendering and interaction, involving core system modules and using interoperable Native API calls. It's clear that the development environment still has much room for improvement. Compared to traditional development, a complete toolchain and third-party libraries are significant advantages. However, Google also uses the same Flutter design in its operating system Fuchsia, and the underlying Skia engine is used in the well-known Chrome browser. Is the future going to be dominated by Google's "Web" platform?

### Update

Flutter requires at least a 6.3MB increase in package size on Android and around 14MB on iOS. Furthermore, due to its independent rendering engine, Flutter's memory usage is currently not ideal. In iOS builds, it consumes around 100MB of memory, and even with a singleton pattern, memory isn't released after the page is closed. This results in shortcomings in app performance, stability, size, and development environment.
